question.name: 1 Fundamentals
question.text:
1.1 Fundamentals

  • (a)

  • (b)

  • (c)

Define (unambiguously) a fluid.

<1>

Help

Show Answer

An instructor-led video working through the problem, auto-pausing when it's your turn to work. Good if you don't know where to start, or are stuck half-way through.
Check your own answer before revealing the answer. Revealing the answer takes away the chance to get it right yourself.
Only view worked solutions after making your best independent attempt. Seeing solutions ruins the learning opportunity. If you are stuck half-way, try (hard!) to only reveal necessary steps before continuing independently.

A substance that deforms continuously under the application of shear stress

Fluids are all around you in everyday life, give examples.

<2>

Help

Show Answer

An instructor-led video working through the problem, auto-pausing when it's your turn to work. Good if you don't know where to start, or are stuck half-way through.
Check your own answer before revealing the answer. Revealing the answer takes away the chance to get it right yourself.
Only view worked solutions after making your best independent attempt. Seeing solutions ruins the learning opportunity. If you are stuck half-way, try (hard!) to only reveal necessary steps before continuing independently.

Air, water, yogurt, petrol, diesel, oil, natural gas(methane), beer, wine, beverages in general, helium, treacle, blood, sweat, tears, sperm, snake poison, vacinations, etc

How do the fluids identified above differ? Contrast by comparing their physical properties, e.g.viscosity, density. [Give units in each case]

<3>

Help

Show Answer

An instructor-led video working through the problem, auto-pausing when it's your turn to work. Good if you don't know where to start, or are stuck half-way through.
Check your own answer before revealing the answer. Revealing the answer takes away the chance to get it right yourself.
Only view worked solutions after making your best independent attempt. Seeing solutions ruins the learning opportunity. If you are stuck half-way, try (hard!) to only reveal necessary steps before continuing independently.

- density differ: \([\rho ]=\frac{M}{{L}^{3}}\)
- viscosity differ: \([\mathcal{M}]=\frac{M}{LT}\)
- composition differs: e.g. air is composed of nitrogen(78%), oxygen(21%), Argon(0.4%), Carbon Dioxide(0.04%), etc.

Q1 Comments

View/make comments; ask/answer questions. Give feedback on the questions to help us improve. Comments are anonymous.

answers.answer: A substance that deforms continuously under the application of shear stress
Air, water, yogurt, petrol, diesel, oil, natural gas(methane), beer, wine, beverages in general, helium, treacle, blood, sweat, tears, sperm, snake poison, vacinations, etc

- density differ: \([\rho ]=\frac{M}{{L}^{3}}\)
- viscosity differ: \([\mathcal{M}]=\frac{M}{LT}\)
- composition differs: e.g. air is composed of nitrogen(78%), oxygen(21%), Argon(0.4%), Carbon Dioxide(0.04%), etc.

question.name: 2 Volume
question.text:
1.2 Volume

Using volume integrals, determine the volume and mass of a fluid of constant desity \(\rho\) in the following containers filled up to the top. Determine the integrals by summing thin slices of height d\(z\) over the \(z\)-direction.

  • (a)

  • (b)

  • (c)

The triangular prism:

V =

<1>

m =

<2>

Help

Equation Help

Show Answer

Worked Solutions

An instructor-led video working through the problem, auto-pausing when it's your turn to work. Good if you don't know where to start, or are stuck half-way through.
Check your own answer before revealing the answer. Revealing the answer takes away the chance to get it right yourself.
Only view worked solutions after making your best independent attempt. Seeing solutions ruins the learning opportunity. If you are stuck half-way, try (hard!) to only reveal necessary steps before continuing independently.

How to input expressions

The equation questions use Maple syntax to evaluate expressions. As with Python, basic operators use the symbols  + - * / ^ and parentheses can be used to structure your expressions. Some common functions can also be used such as sqrt(),  sin() and arcsin(). To get the natural exponential, use exp() as e is not intepreted as Euler's number.

For Greek letters, type the full name of the letter. For example mu*U  will render \( \mu U \) (there is no need to use a back slash).

Click the to preview your expressions.

For example, writing exp(i*pi) - 1 will produce:

\( \text{e}^{i \pi} - 1 \)

The volume can be determined by: \(V=\int_{D}^{}dV\)

⬆ Previous Step ⬆
⬇ Next Step ⬇
Show All

Taking a horizontal slice of thickness \(dz\):
\(dV=WB(z)dz\)

Using convergence of triangles :
\(\frac{B(z)}{z}=\frac{b}{h}\)

The mass can be determined by:
\(m=\int_{D}^{}dm\), where \(dm\) is element of mass defined by \(dm=\rho dV\)

Since \(\rho\) is constant , \(m=\rho\int_{D}^{}dV=\rho V\)

Substitute in volume equation from earlier

\(m=\frac{\rho}{2}wbh\)

\(m=\frac{\rho}{2}wbh \\ m=\frac{1}{2}wbh\)

The truncated cone:

V =

<3>

m =

<4>

Help

Equation Help

Show Answer

Worked Solutions

An instructor-led video working through the problem, auto-pausing when it's your turn to work. Good if you don't know where to start, or are stuck half-way through.
Check your own answer before revealing the answer. Revealing the answer takes away the chance to get it right yourself.
Only view worked solutions after making your best independent attempt. Seeing solutions ruins the learning opportunity. If you are stuck half-way, try (hard!) to only reveal necessary steps before continuing independently.

How to input expressions

The equation questions use Maple syntax to evaluate expressions. As with Python, basic operators use the symbols  + - * / ^ and parentheses can be used to structure your expressions. Some common functions can also be used such as sqrt(),  sin() and arcsin(). To get the natural exponential, use exp() as e is not intepreted as Euler's number.

For Greek letters, type the full name of the letter. For example mu*U  will render \( \mu U \) (there is no need to use a back slash).

Click the to preview your expressions.

For example, writing exp(i*pi) - 1 will produce:

\( \text{e}^{i \pi} - 1 \)

Again consider a horizontal slice of thickness \(dz\). An element of volume is given by:
\(dV=\pi R(z)^{2}dz\)

⬆ Previous Step ⬆
⬇ Next Step ⬇
Show All

To find R(z) we use that \(R(0)=R_{0}\) and \(R(h)=R_{t}\) and the fact that R grows linearly, implying that:
\(R(z)=R_{0}+\frac{R_{t}-R_{0}}{h} z\)

The volume can be determined by:
\(\begin{aligned}V &=\pi \int_{0}^{h}\left\{\frac{R_{t}-R_{0}}{h} z+R_{0} \right\}^{2} d z \\&=\pi \int_{0}^{h}\left\{\left(\frac{R_{t}-R_{0}}{h}\right)^{2} z^{2}+2 \frac{R_{0}}{h}\left(R_{t}-R_{0}\right) z+R_{0}^{2}\right\} d z \\&=\pi\left\{\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{R_{t}-R_{0}}{h}\right)^{2} z^{3}+\frac{R_{0}}{h}\left(R_{t}-R_{0}\right) z^{2}+\left.R_{0}^{2} z\right|_{0} ^{h}\right\} \\V &=\frac{1}{3} \pi R_{0}^{2} h+\frac{1}{3} \pi h R_{t} R_{0}+\frac{1}{3} \pi h R_{t}^{2} \quad \cdots \quad E q .(3)\end{aligned}\)

Multiplying Eq(3) with \(\frac{R_{t}-R_{0}}{R_{t}-R_{0}}\) gives us a different form of the equation:
\(\begin{aligned}&V=\frac{1}{3} \pi R_{t}^{2} h\left(\frac{R_{t}}{R_{t-} R_{0}}\right)-\frac{1}{3} \pi R_{0}^{2} h\left(\frac{R_{0}}{R_{t}-R_{0}}\right) \\&V=\frac{1}{3} \pi R_{t}^{2}\left\{h+\frac{h R_{0}}{R_{t}-R_{0}}\right\}-\frac{1}{3} \pi R_{0}^{2}\left\{\frac{h}{R_{t}-R_{t}}\right\} \quad \cdots \quad E q .(4)\end{aligned}\)

This equation (4) is equivalent to the subtraction of volume of cone 1 with volume of cone 2
\(\begin{aligned}&V_{\text {cone } 1}=\int_{-h^{*}}^{h^{*}+h} \pi R(z)^{2} d z \\&V_{\text {cone } 2}=\int_{-h^{*}}^{0} \pi R(z)^{2} d z, \text { where } h^{*}=\frac{h R_{0}}{R_{t}-R_{0}}\end{aligned}\)

In the same way to determine the mass of the fluid in the container of problem 6a and as the density of the fluid is constant, the mass is given by:
\(m=\rho \int d v\)

\(m=\rho\left\{\frac{1}{3} \pi R_{0}{ }^{2} h+\frac{1}{3} \pi h R_{t} R_{0}+\frac{1}{3} \pi h R_{t}^{2}\right\}\)

\(V=\frac{1}{3} \pi R_{t}^{2}\left\{h+\frac{h R_{0}}{R_{t}-R_{0}}\right\}-\frac{1}{3} \pi R_{0}^{2}\left\{\frac{h}{R t-R_{i}}\right\} \\ m=\rho\left\{\frac{1}{3} \pi R_{0}{ }^{2} h+\frac{1}{3} \pi h R_{t} R_{0}+\frac{1}{3} \pi h R_{t}^{2}\right\}\)

Using the geometries of the previous question, calculate the mass of the fluid if the fluid density varies linearly according to \(\rho(z)=\rho_{0}-a z\)

W =

<5>

Help

Equation Help

Show Answer

Worked Solutions

An instructor-led video working through the problem, auto-pausing when it's your turn to work. Good if you don't know where to start, or are stuck half-way through.
Check your own answer before revealing the answer. Revealing the answer takes away the chance to get it right yourself.
Only view worked solutions after making your best independent attempt. Seeing solutions ruins the learning opportunity. If you are stuck half-way, try (hard!) to only reveal necessary steps before continuing independently.

How to input expressions

The equation questions use Maple syntax to evaluate expressions. As with Python, basic operators use the symbols  + - * / ^ and parentheses can be used to structure your expressions. Some common functions can also be used such as sqrt(),  sin() and arcsin(). To get the natural exponential, use exp() as e is not intepreted as Euler's number.

For Greek letters, type the full name of the letter. For example mu*U  will render \( \mu U \) (there is no need to use a back slash).

Click the to preview your expressions.

For example, writing exp(i*pi) - 1 will produce:

\( \text{e}^{i \pi} - 1 \)

If the density of the fluid varies linearly according to :
\(\rho(z)=\rho_{0}-a z\)

⬆ Previous Step ⬆
⬇ Next Step ⬇
Show All

a) The weight of the fluid in the container of problem 6. a is given as:
\(\begin{aligned}W &=\int \rho(z) g d V \\&=\int_{0}^{h}\left(\rho_{0}-a z\right) g \frac{b w}{h} z d z \\&= g w b h \left\{\frac{1}{2} \rho_{0} z^{2}-\left.\frac{a}{3} z^{3}\right|_{0} ^{h}\right\} \\W &=g w b h\left\{\frac{1}{2} \rho_{0}-\frac{a}{3} h\right\} \text { where a is the gravitational acceleration }\end{aligned}\)

b) The weight of the fluid in the container of problem 6.b is given as:
\(\begin{aligned}W &=\int_{0}^{h}\left(\rho_{0}-a z\right) g \pi\left\{\frac{R_{t}-R_{0}}{h} z+R_{0}\right\}^{2} d z \\&=\rho_{0} g \pi \int_{0}^{h}\left\{\left(\frac{R_{t}-R_{0}}{h}\right)^{2} z^{2}+2 \frac{R_{0}}{h}\left(R_{t}-R_{0}\right) z+R_{0}^{2}\right\} d z\\&-a g \pi \int_{0}^{h}\left\{\left(\frac{R_{t}-R_{0}}{h}\right)^{2} z^{3}+2 \frac{R_{0}}{h}\left(R_{t}-R_{0}\right) z^{2}+R_{0}^{2} z\right\} d z\\&=\rho_{0} g \pi\left\{\left(\frac{R_{t}-R_{0}}{h}\right)^{2} \frac{1}{3} z^{3}+\frac{R_{0}}{h}\left(R_{t}-R_{0}\right) z^{2}+\left.R_{0}^{2} z\right|_{0} ^{h}\right\}\&-a g \pi\left\{\frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{R_{t}-R_{0}}{h}\right)^{2} z^{4}+\frac{2}{3} \frac{R_{0}}{h}\left(R_{t}-R_{0}\right) z^{3}+\left.\frac{R_{0}^{2}}{2} z^{2}\right|_{0} ^{h}\right\}\&W=\rho_{0} g \pi h\left\{\frac{1}{3} R_{t}^{2}+\frac{1}{3} R_{t} R_{0}+\frac{1}{3} R_{0}^{2}\right\}-a g \pi h^{2}\left\{\frac{1}{4} R_{t}^{2}+\frac{1}{6} R_{t} R_{0}+\frac{1}{12} R_{0}^{2}\right\}\end{aligned}\)

\(W=\frac{1}{3} \rho_{0} g \pi h\left\{\left(R_{t}+R_{0}\right)^{2}-R_{t} R_{0}\right\}-\frac{1}{4} \operatorname{ag\pi h}^{2}\left\{\left(R_{t}+R_{0}\right)^{2}-\frac{2}{3}\left(R_{t} R_{0}+R_{0}^{2}\right)\right\}\)

\(W=\frac{1}{3} \rho_{0} g \pi h\left\{\left(R_{t}+R_{0}\right)^{2}-R_{t} R_{0}\right\}-\frac{1}{4} \operatorname{ag\pi h}^{2}\left\{\left(R_{t}+R_{0}\right)^{2}-\frac{2}{3}\left(R_{t} R_{0}+R_{0}^{2}\right)\right\}\)

Q2 Comments

View/make comments; ask/answer questions. Give feedback on the questions to help us improve. Comments are anonymous.
